title: Adsorptive control of water in esterification with immobilized enzymes: II. Fixed-bed reactor behavior
abstract:
Adsorptive Control of Water in
Esterification with Immobilized Enzymes:
II. Fixed-Bed Reactor Behavior
Paul Mensah, John L. Gainer, Giorgio Carta
Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Virginia,
Charlottesville, Virginia 22903-2442; telephone: 804-924-6281; fax:
804-982-2658; e-mail: gc@Virginia.edu
Received 14 November 1997; accepted 30 April 1998
Abstract: Experimental and theoretical studies are con-
ducted to understand the dynamic behavior of a continu-
ous-flow fixed-bed reactor in which an esterification is
catalyzed by an immobilized enzyme in an organic sol-
vent medium. The experimental system consists of a
commercial immobilized lipase preparation known as Li-
pozyme as the biocatalyst, with propionic acid and iso-
amyl alcohol (dissolved in hexane) as the reaction sub-
strates. A complex dynamic behavior is observed experi-
mentally as a result of the simultaneous occurrence of
reaction and adsorption phenomena. Both propionic acid
and water are adsorbed by the biocatalyst resulting in
lower reaction rates. In addition, an excessive accumu-
lation of water in the reactor leads to a rapid irreversible
inactivation of the enzyme. A model based on previ-
ously-obtained adsorption isotherms and kinetic expres-
sions, as well as on adsorption rate measurements ob-
tained in this work, is used to predict the concentration
and thermodynamic activity of water along the reactor
length. The model successfully predicts the dynamic be-
havior of the reactor and shows that a maximum ther-
modynamic activity of water occurs at a point at some
distance from the reactor entrance. A cation exchange
resin in sodium form, packed in the reactor as a selective
water adsorbent together with the catalyst particles, is
shown to be an effective means for preventing an exces-
sive accumulation of water formed in the reaction. Its use
results in longer cycle times and greater productivity. As
predicted by the model, the experimental results show
that the water adsorbed on the catalyst and on the ion
exchange resin can be removed with isoamyl alcohol
with no apparent loss in enzyme activity. © 1998 John
Wiley & Sons, Inc. Biotechnol Bioeng 60: 445–453, 1998.
Keywords: immobilized enzymes; organic solvents; es-
terification; water; continuous flow reactor; adsorption
modeling
INTRODUCTION
In Part I of this work (Mensah et al., 1998) we investigated
the dynamic behavior of batch esterifications using immo-
bilized enzymes in organic solvents and the removal of
water formed in the reaction by means of adsorption onto an
ion-exchange resin. The adsorptive control of water accu-
mulation in a batch reaction system was shown experimen-
tally to yield (1) higher reaction rates, and (2) improved life
of the biocatalyst. Several issues need to be considered in
dealing with such systems. First, when using immobilized
enzymes, because the supports used are often hydrophilic
(see, for example, Ison et al., 1994), it is necessary to si-
multaneously consider adsorption of water on the enzyme
and on the support matrix, as well as on any added adsor-
bent media. Second, these systems are highly non-ideal
from a thermodynamic viewpoint, so that partitioning of
water between the different phases must be expressed in
terms of thermodynamic activity rather than concentration
(Halling, 1990, 1994; Mazzotti et al., 1997a,b; Valivety et
al., 1992). Because, in turn, the thermodynamic activity of
water varies both with the nature of the organic solvent and
the nature and concentration of the reaction substrates
(Kvittingen et al., 1992), large variations in water partition-
ing can occur in esterifications as the more polar acid and
alcohol substrates are converted into the less polar ester
product. As a result, complicated changes are observed for
the water concentration in batch esterifications. Third, mass
transfer resistances may be important for certain conditions
both for substrates diffusing within the immobilized bio-
catalyst (Dumont et al., 1992; Indlekofer et al., 1996) and
for water adsorption.
A model incorporating reaction kinetics, water partition-
ing, and mass transfer effects was developed in Part I of this
work (Mensah et al., 1998) to predict the behavior of batch
enzymatic reactors. However, in the practical operation of
large-scale enzymatic syntheses, continuous-flow packed-
bed reactors are often preferred (Carta et al., 1991; Indle-
kofer et al., 1993, 1996; Marty et al., 1992, 1994). In such
systems, gradients in substrate and product concentrations
are formed along the reactor, making it difficult to predict
the conversion attainable without an understanding of the
interplay of reaction and partitioning phenomena. In par-
ticular, it is difficult to predict how water concentration and
water thermodynamic activity profiles develop along the
reactor in esterifications or other reactions that produce wa-
ter. Moreover, because water tends to continuously accu-
Correspondence to: Giorgio Carta
Contract grant sponsors: National Science Foundation; Academic En-
hancement Program of the University of Virginia
Contract grant number: BCS-9301447
© 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
CCC 0006-3592/98/040445-09
 mulate in the reactor, it is necessary to provide for its pe-
riodic removal. Generally, adequate reaction-regeneration
strategies are difficult to establish a priori without a quan-
titative modeling approach. Thus, the main objective of this
article is to develop an understanding of the development of
concentration profiles in packed-bed reactors for esterifica-
tions with immobilized enzymes, and to provide an ap-
proach for quantitative predictions. The Lipozyme-
catalyzed esterification of isoamyl alcohol and propionic
acid in a hexane solvent is used as a model system. A model
based on kinetics and sorption equilibrium results from Part
I (Mensah et al., 1998) and on additional data obtained in
packed-beds is presented as a tool for design and optimiza-
tion of reactor operations. The effects of adding an ion-
exchange resin as a selective water adsorbent to improve the
water-adsorptive capacity of the reactor are also studied
both experimentally and via model simulations.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Lipozyme IM (Novo Nordisk Bioindustrials, Inc., Frank-
linton, NC) was used as the biocatalyst and the strong-acid
cation exchange resin Dowex HCR-W2 in sodium form
(The Dow Chemical Company, Midland, MI) was used as a
water adsorbent. In sodium form, the resin is inert with
respect to reaction and capable of selectively adsorbing wa-
ter from relatively non-polar solutions. General relevant
properties of these materials and pretreatment procedures
are given in Part I of this work (Mensah et al., 1998). Two
batches of Lipozyme with somewhat different properties
were used in this work. The first batch was the same as that
used in Part I. The second batch was essentially the same in
all respects but had a lower initial water content of about 1.1
mmol/g (compared to 2.4 mmol/g for the first batch) and a
somewhat higher maximum activity ro
m 4 3.6 mmol/h g
(compared to 2.4 mmol/h g for the first batch). Interestingly,
however, batch reaction tests showed that except for the
difference in maximum rate, the kinetic behavior as a func-
tion of substrate concentration was indistinguishable for the
two biocatalyst samples.
Experimental work was carried out in glass chromato-
graphic columns 1.5 cm in diameter (Omnifit, Toms River,
NJ). Lipozyme alone, Dowex HCR-W2/Na form, or mix-
tures thereof were packed by pouring in the column a pre-
determined amount of each material in dry form. Two Eldex
(Model A-60-S, Napa, CA) metering pumps were used to
supply solvent and reaction mixtures to the column. Typical
flow rates were 0.1–1 cm3/min with 4–12 g of Lipozyme
and typical feed concentrations for reaction experiments
were 0.2–1 mol/L each of propionic acid and isoamyl alco-
hol in hexane. For cyclic experiments, accumulated water
was removed from the catalyst and adsorbent by feeding
pure isoamyl alcohol. Samples of the column effluent were
collected in sealable glass vials and analyzed off-line by gas
chromatography (Mensah, 1997). Solvents and reaction
substrates, obtained from Sigma Chemical Company (St.
Louis, MO) were generally kept anhydrous by storing them
with 3-Å molecular sieves. All experiments were carried out
at room temperature, 22 ± 2°C.
The void fraction of columns packed with Lipozyme was
obtained by measuring the retention time of a step change
from hexane to a mixture of n-octane in hexane. A void
fraction of 0.72 ± 0.01 was found from these experiments.
Assuming an extraparticle porosity «b 4 0.4, this value
corresponds to an intraparticle porosity «p 4 0.53 ± 0.02.
For columns packed with the Dowex resin which is gel-type
and, thus, does not have a macroporosity, the column void
fraction is due to the extraparticle contribution and was
about 0.4. Thus, for columns packed with both Lipozyme
and the Dowex resin, the column porosity was estimated
from:
« 4 «b + (1 −«b)«pfc
(1)
where fc is the volume fraction of Lipozyme in the packed
bed and «b 4 0.4.
RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
Fixed-Bed Reaction Behavior
Typical effluent profiles for a column containing fresh Li-
pozyme are shown in Figure 1a. Isoamyl alcohol and the
isoamyl propionate produced in the reactor emerge at a time
corresponding approximately to the column void volume.
The alcohol concentration rapidly increases to a maximum
then decreases back to a minimum before gradually increas-
ing again. The ester concentration increases more gradually
to a maximum that corresponds to the minimum of the
alcohol. Then, it decreases at about the same rate as that at
which the alcohol increases. Propionic acid begins to break-
through at the time where the alcohol concentration has a
minimum. Following a fairly sharp breakthrough, the acid
continues to increase more gradually following the alcohol
profile. Water begins to breakthrough some time after the
acid, and its concentration increases slowly while ester pro-
duction gradually declines. The maximum instantaneous
conversion in this experiment is about 73%. However, it is
apparent that a steady state is never reached as evidenced by
the fact that a stoichiometric balance between reactants and
products does not hold.
The complexity of reactor behavior shown in this figure
is a result of the interplay of reaction and adsorption effects.
Both propionic acid and water are adsorbed by the biocata-
lyst. Although the capacity for water is greater, the sorption
isotherm is more favorable for propionic acid (see Figs. 6
and 8 in Mensah et al., 1998). As a result, ester is initially
formed only near the reactor entrance where both isoamyl
alcohol and propionic acid are present. Because only pro-
pionic acid and water are adsorbed, the unreacted alcohol
and the ester produced by the reaction move ahead of the
propionic acid adsorption front and exit the reactor. Water,
on the other hand, being retained by the biocatalyst, remains
largely behind the acid adsorption front. As a result, no
reaction takes place downstream of this front. As propionic
446

pozyme are shown in Figure 1a. Isoamyl alcohol and the
isoamyl propionate produced in the reactor emerge at a time
corresponding approximately to the column void volume.

then decreases back to a minimum before gradually increas-
ing again. The ester concentration increases more gradually
to a maximum that corresponds to the minimum of the
alcohol. Then, it decreases at about the same rate as that at
which the alcohol increases. Propionic acid begins to break-
through at the time where the alcohol concentration has a
minimum. Following a fairly sharp breakthrough, the acid
continues to increase more gradually following the alcohol
profile. Water begins to breakthrough some time after the
acid, and its concentration increases slowly while ester pro-

The complexity of reactor behavior shown in this figure
is a result of the interplay of reaction and adsorption effects.
Both propionic acid and water are adsorbed by the biocata-
lyst. Although the capacity for water is greater, the sorption
isotherm is more favorable for propionic acid (see Figs. 6
and 8 in Mensah et al., 1998). As a result, ester is initially
formed only near the reactor entrance where both isoamyl
alcohol and propionic acid are present. Because only pro-
pionic acid and water are adsorbed, the unreacted alcohol
and the ester produced by the reaction move ahead of the
propionic acid adsorption front and exit the reactor. Water,
on the other hand, being retained by the biocatalyst, remains
largely behind the acid adsorption front. As a result, no
reaction takes place downstream of this front. As propionic
BIOTECHNOLOGY AND BIOENGINEERING, VOL. 60, NO. 4, NOVEMBER 20, 1998
acid moves down the packed bed, the reaction occurs over
a greater portion of the reactor. At the acid breakthrough,
ester formation occurs throughout the reactor and a maxi-
mum conversion is achieved. Water produced in the reac-
tion, however, continues to accumulate on the biocatalyst,
causing, in turn, a gradual decline in the rate of reaction, as
shown in Part I of this work (Mensah et al., 1998). Thus, the
conversion gradually declines to a low value. It can be noted
that because of the stoichiometric relationship and since the
ester is not significantly adsorbed, the area between the ester
concentration profile and the water concentration profile in
Figure 1a is representative of the total amount of water
adsorbed in the reactor. This amounts to approximately 100
mmol for the experiment shown in this figure.
In an attempt to restore the initial enzymatic activity,
immediately following the run shown in Figure 1a, the re-
actor was flushed with about 2 bed volumes of hexane to
remove reactants and products from the interstitial voids,
and then with isoamyl alcohol to remove adsorbed water.
Some water (0.38 mol/L) was added to the isoamyl alcohol
feed to provide an equilibrium water content of the biocata-
lyst close to the value of the fresh Lipozyme (2.4 mmol/g).
The water elution profile obtained is shown in Figure 1b.
The profile is asymmetric as a result of the favorable nature
of the sorption isotherm for these conditions. The amount of
water eluted after about 8 h (obtained by integration of the
effluent profile) is slightly larger (about 110 mmol) than the
water accumulated in the preceding reaction run. The dif-
ference is due to additional ester formed when the isoamyl
alcohol fed to the column reacts with adsorbed propionic
acid. The reactor was then flushed again with 3–4 bed vol-
umes of hexane and used in a second run whose resulting
profiles are shown in Figure 1c. It is evident that while the
general shape of the concentration profiles is similar to the
first run, the activity of the biocatalyst is greatly reduced.
The maximum conversion obtained was only about 30%
and declined to less than 20% after 7 h of operation (∼9 bed
volumes). Further attempts to regenerate the biocatalyst
with isoamyl alcohol containing various amounts of water
from 0 to 0.6 mol/L were unsuccessful, yielding maximum
To operate the reactor in a stable cycle of reaction and
regeneration steps we found that it was necessary to halt the
substrate feed to the reactor prior to the formation of a
water-saturated solution in the effluent. For example, Fig-
ures 2a–c show the results obtained when the reactor was
operated by stopping the reactant feed before a significant
breakthrough of water occurred. Starting with fresh catalyst,
a maximum conversion level of about 80% is obtained in
the initial run (Fig. 2a). After 4 h of reaction time (∼3 bed
volumes), and before any water could be detected in the
effluent, the reactor feed was halted and regeneration was
performed first by flushing the reactor with hexane and then
by removing accumulated water with isoamyl alcohol con-
taining 0.63 mol/L for 6 h (∼5 bed volumes). The water
effluent profile is shown in Figure 2b. A new reaction run
was then conducted after flushing the reactor again with
hexane to remove the excess alcohol. The results are shown
in Figure 2c. The only significant difference between these
profiles and those in Figure 2a, is a slightly higher alcohol
peak, perhaps resulting from some residual isoamyl alcohol
that was not completely removed by flushing the reactor
with hexane. The cycle shown in Figure 2 with a 3–4 h
reaction time followed by a 4–6 h isoamyl alcohol regen-
eration was repeated 8 times over a 3-month period with the
same reactor without any detectable loss of activity (Men-
sah, 1997). During these runs, varying the water content of
the isoamyl alcohol used for regeneration from 0 to 0.63
mol/L had no effect as long as in each case regeneration was
initiated prior to water breakthrough.
It is apparent from these results that the biocatalyst be-
Effluent profiles for Lipozyme catalyzed esterification of 1
mol/L propionic acid and isoamyl alcohol in hexane in a fixed-bed reactor.
Lipozyme weight 4 12 g, bed length 4 19 cm, flow rate 4 0.47 cm3/min.
(a) Reaction run with fresh Lipozyme, (b) water elution profile for regen-
eration with isoamyl alcohol containing 0.38 mol/L water, (c) reaction run
after removal of adsorbed water with isoamyl alcohol.
MENSAH, GAINER, AND CARTA: CONTROL OF WATER IN FIXED-BED ENZYMATIC ESTERIFICATION
comes irreversibly deactivated when propionic acid and an
excess water are present simultaneously. In Figure 1a, for
example, at the end of the experiment the effluent contains
approximately 0.9 mol/L propionic acid together with water
at a concentration of about 0.38 mol/L. This water concen-
tration corresponds closely to the saturation value calculated
from the UNIFAC model (Gmehling et al., 1982; Hansen et
al., 1991) for the composition of the effluent, as is discussed
in Part I of this work (Mensah et al., 1998). This indicates
that the water adsorption capacity of the biocatalyst has
been exceeded and that a bulk water phase was likely pre-
sent in the reactor. Further evidence that the biocatalyst is
deactivated by the simultaneous presence of propionic acid
and a bulk water phase was obtained in batch studies as
follows. A 0.2 g Lipozyme sample was incubated for 24 h
with 2 cm3 of a mixture containing 1 mol/L propionic acid
and 0.5 mol/L water in hexane. This amount of water ex-
ceeds the solubility limit. No catalytic activity was observed
after attempting to regenerate this enzyme sample with iso-
amyl alcohol. In contrast, nearly 100% of the initial activity
was recovered when a Lipozyme sample was incubated with
1 mol/L propionic acid in hexane but without an excess
water, or when the latter was incubated with isoamyl alco-
hol solutions containing 0–2 mol/L water. As shown by
several authors (Stevenson and Storer, 1991; Halling, 1994)
partitioning of polar substrates into an aqueous phase sur-
rounding the enzyme can lead to its denaturation. In our
reactor studies, it is likely that propionic acid caused an
acidification of an aqueous environment that surrounded the
enzyme when an excess of water was allowed to accumulate
Prediction of Fixed-Bed Reactor Behavior
Although in some cases it is possible empirically to find
conditions that permit a stable operation of the reactor, in
general, it is desirable to utilize a model to predict the
reactant and product concentrations, especially that of wa-
ter. The axially-dispersed plug-flow model with the linear
driving force (LDF) approximation (LeVan et al., 1997) was
used in this work. Such a model, although of a semiempiri-
cal nature, generally produces qualitatively and quantita-
tively correct results in the description of adsorptive and
reactive systems. If we consider a packed bed containing
both the catalyst and an adsorbent, the following conserva-
tion equations and boundary conditions can be written:
where rc and ra are the mass of catalyst and of adsorbent
particle per unit bed volume, Ci is the fluid-phase concen-
centrations in the catalyst and adsorbent, respectively, DL is
the axial dispersion coefficient, and kc
adsorption rate coefficients for the catalyst and adsorbent
particles. The starred quantities refer to equilibrium values
Effluent profiles for Lipozyme catalyzed esterification of 1.1
mol/L propionic acid and isoamyl alcohol in hexane in a fixed-bed reactor
stopping substrate feed prior to water breakthrough. Lipozyme weight 4
4 g, bed length 4 6 cm, flow rate 4 0.14 cm3/min. (a) Reaction run with
fresh Lipozyme, (b) water elution profile for regeneration with isoamyl
alcohol containing 0.63 mol/L water, (c) reaction run after removal of
adsorbed water with isoamyl alcohol.
BIOTECHNOLOGY AND BIOENGINEERING, VOL. 60, NO. 4, NOVEMBER 20, 1998
w)f(CRCOOH,CR8OH) represents the
rate of reaction. As shown in Part I (Mensah et al., 1998),
the reaction term comprises three elements: the stoichiomet-
ric coefficient ni, the maximum rate coefficient rm(qc
varies with the amount of water adsorbed on the biocatalyst,
and the function f(CRCOOH,CR8OH) that represents the effects
of substrate concentrations on the reaction rate. Because of
the nonlinearities associated with equilibrium and rate ex-
pressions, a numerical solution of these equations is re-
quired in the general case. This was obtained by orthogonal
collocation using a method similar to that described by
To predict the reactor behavior quantitatively, Eqs. (2)–
(4) are written for each species along with pre-established
adsorption equilibrium and kinetic expressions. In our ex-
perimental system, water is adsorbed both on Lipozyme and
on the Dowex resin while propionic acid is adsorbed only
on Lipozyme. The other species, isoamyl alcohol and iso-
amyl propionate, are not adsorbed significantly (Mensah et
al., 1998). Thus, for these species qc
CR8OH) as well as equilibrium isotherms for water on Lipo-
zyme and the Dowex resin and for propionic acid on Lipo-
zyme are given in Part I of this article (Mensah et al., 1998).
The equilibrium isotherms are expressed in terms of ther-
modynamic activities which, in turn, are calculated as a
function of solution composition using the UNIFAC model
with the parameters given by Hansen et al. (1991). Finally,
the axial dispersion coefficient and adsorption rate param-
eters were obtained as follows. For the determination of DL,
we matched the numerical solution of Eq. (2) to experimen-
tal breakthrough curves for solutions of n-octane in hexane.
Because n-octane is not significantly adsorbed, its break-
through behavior is dominated by axial dispersion effects. A
value of DL 4 6.9 10−4 cm2/s was found at a superficial
fluid velocity of 1.9 10−3 cm/s with a column packed with
Lipozyme particles. This value corresponds to a Peclet num-
ber Pe 4 udp/«bDL ∼0.3, which is similar in magnitude to
values expected for typical packed beds (LeVan et al.,
1997). The same empirical Peclet number was used for
different fluid velocities in accordance with the behavior
expected on the basis of available correlations in terms of
Reynolds and Schmidt numbers (LeVan et al., 1997).
The LDF rate coefficients for the adsorbed species were
found by matching the numerical solution of Eqs. (2) and
(3) or Eqs. (2) and (4) to single-component breakthrough
curves obtained with columns packed with Lipozyme for
water and for propionic acid, and with the Dowex resin for
water. Typical results comparing experimental and fitted
breakthrough curves are shown in Figure 3 for the adsorp-
tion of water from isoamyl alcohol on Lipozyme and the
Dowex resin, and in Figure 4 for the adsorption of propionic
acid and water from hexane on Lipozyme and for water
from hexane on the Dowex resin. The adsorption capacity of
the Dowex resin for water is much greater than that of
Lipozyme. However, a shallower curve is obtained in the
latter case, indicating slower mass transfer kinetics. The
propionic acid breakthrough curve for adsorption on Lipo-
zyme also has a distinctive shape with a rapid initial rise,
followed by a very gradual approach to the feed concentra-
tion, which is likely a result of the very favorable nature of
the adsorption isotherm. In all cases, however, it appears
that the LDF model provides a reasonable fit of the break-
through curves. A summary of the fitted-rate parameters is
given in Table I. The value of Pe determined from the
n-octane curves was used for these calculations. However,
the effects of axial dispersion are practically negligible for

MENSAH, GAINER, AND CARTA: CONTROL OF WATER IN FIXED-BED ENZYMATIC ESTERIFICATION
the adsorbed species because mass transfer resistances tend
to dominate overall dispersion in this case. Remarkably, the
rate coefficients for water in each material are the same
irrespective of the solvent, indicating that the latter does not
affect the rate of intraparticle transport to a significant ex-
tent. On the other hand, there are large differences in the
rate coefficients for Lipozyme and for the Dowex resin, the
latter being much smaller even if the mean particle size of
the two materials is essentially the same (dp ∼0.04 cm). The
difference may be due to the different structure of these
materials. Lipozyme is a macroporous medium, and trans-
port of water is likely limited by pore diffusion (Indlekofer
et al., 1996). Conversely, the Dowex resin is gel-type, and
diffusion occurs through the polymer mesh which, for the
conditions of our experiments, is only slightly swollen
(Sinegra and Carta, 1987). It is also noteworthy that the rate
coefficient for adsorption of propionic acid on Lipozyme is
much smaller than the corresponding value for water (the
fact that it has the same value as for the adsorption of water
on the Dowex resin is purely coincidental). As also sug-
gested by the rather different adsorption equilibrium behav-
ior (Mensah et al., 1998), this indicates that the two species
are adsorbed, likely, at different sites within the biocatalyst
The rate parameters in Table I along with adsorption
equilibrium and kinetic expressions obtained in Part I of this
work (Mensah et al., 1998) were used to predict the reaction
runs. Predicted and experimental effluent profiles for a re-
actor packed with Lipozyme alone are shown in Figure 5a.
The effluent profiles obtained during the following isoamyl
alcohol regeneration step are shown in Figure 5b. This run
corresponds to conditions where the reactor was operated in
a stable cycle of reaction followed by water removal with
isoamyl alcohol. Generally, experimental and predicted pro-
files are in excellent agreement. In particular, during the
reaction step the model correctly predicts the occurrence of
a maximum and a minimum in the isoamyl alcohol profile
and the breakthrough times of propionic acid and water. The
model also predicts the gradual decline in the isoamyl pro-
pionate concentration that occurs as a result of accumulation
of water on the biocatalyst. The maximum conversion is
slightly underestimated, however. This is attributed to the
presence of some residual isoamyl alcohol at the beginning
of the reaction run left over from the preceding regenera-
tion. A peak isoamyl alcohol concentration higher than pre-
dicted is also obtained for the same reason. A good agree-
ment also exists between predicted and experimental results
during regeneration with isoamyl alcohol. As is observed
experimentally, the model predicts that additional isoamyl
propionate is formed by reaction between the propionic acid
adsorbed in the reactor during the previous step and the
isoamyl alcohol supplied to remove accumulated water.
Complete conversion of the adsorbed acid is predicted by
the model, and no acid could be detected in the effluent. The
predicted water-elution profile is also in agreement with the
experimental one. The asymmetry of the peak derives
mainly from the nature of the sorption isotherm for this
species (which is favorable for adsorption).
Predicted and experimental concentrations for a run with
Lipozyme alone (as in Figure 5), but using a 5-times lower
substrate concentration, are shown in Figure 6. In this case,
breakthrough of either the acid or water does not occur for
over 30 h. In agreement with the model predictions, break-
through is greatly delayed because conversion is nearly
complete for these conditions. As a result, the fluid phase is
highly hydrophobic (essentially only ester and hexane are
present), and water is strongly retained on the support.
Breakthrough of propionic acid is likewise greatly delayed

as a result of nearly complete conversion in the reactor. The
model slightly underestimates conversion after about 15 h,
which could be due to inaccuracies in the kinetic model at
very low substrate concentrations. The profiles obtained by
regeneration with isoamyl alcohol are also in good agree-
ment with the predicted ones. As in the previous case, some
additional ester is formed. However, the amount is very
small because conversion was nearly complete during the
previous reaction run. Hence, little acid was accumulated in
the reactor. The concentration of water predicted is some-
what higher than observed experimentally. However, the
time required for essentially complete removal of water is
closely approximated by the model.
Predicted and experimental profiles for a reaction run
with a column containing both Lipozyme and the Dowex
resin are shown in Figure 7a. The amount of Lipozyme used
was the same as in Figure 5. Thus, essentially the same
conversion is obtained for short times. Breakthrough of the
acid also occurs at essentially the same time, because this
species is adsorbed only on Lipozyme. Thus, initially, the
acid behavior is unaffected by the presence of the Dowex
resin in the column. Water breakthrough, on the other hand,
is delayed and, as a result, ester production declines less
rapidly because the rate at which water accumulates on the
biocatalyst is reduced. As in the previous case, the model
slightly underestimates the maximum conversion which is
likely a result of the presence of some isoamyl alcohol at the
beginning of the reaction run. The profiles obtained by re-
generating the reactor with isoamyl alcohol are shown in
Figure 7b. Additional ester is again formed from acid ad-
sorbed on the catalyst during the preceding reaction run.
However, a longer time is required to remove all the water
from the reactor both because more water was formed in the
reaction run as well as because both equilibrium and mass
transfer rates are less favorable for desorbing water from the
To understand the effect of water adsorption, it is instruc-
tive to consider the reactant and product concentration pro-
files and the thermodynamic activity of water along the
rector length. As shown previously (Mensah et al., 1998),
the thermodynamic activity of water is directly related to
water accumulation on the biocatalyst. In turn, the latter is

related to the catalytic activity of the immobilized enzyme
system. In particular, for our system, the catalytic activity
decreases rapidly when the thermodynamic activity of water
reaches values above 0.6. Concentrations predicted along
the reactor length for conditions analogous to those of Fig-
ure 5a with Lipozyme alone, and to those of Figure 7a for
a mixture of Lipozyme and the Dowex resin are shown in
Figures 8a and b, respectively. Calculated profiles are
shown at the end of the corresponding experimental reaction
runs (t ∼7 h). In both cases, the water activity has a maxi-
mum at some distance from the reactor entrance. In the case
without Dowex resin, shown in Figure 8a, the water activity
reaches a high value of about 0.94 at a dimensionless reactor
length of 0.5. From the sorption isotherm for water on Li-
pozyme (see Mensah et al., 1998), this corresponds to an
equilibrium water loading on the biocatalyst in excess of 20
mmol/g. For these conditions, the catalytic activity is only
about 20% of what would be obtained with fresh catalyst.
Conversely, for the run with Dowex resin shown in Figure
8b, the maximum water activity, reached at a dimensionless
reactor length of about 0.4, is around 0.65. In this case, by
comparison with the sorption equilibrium isotherms (see
Mensah et al., 1998), the maximum water loading on the
catalyst is less than about 6 mmol/g, because most of the
water formed is retained by the Dowex resin. As a conse-
quence, the catalyst activity remains within about 80% of
the initial value. It is apparent that if the run in Figure 8a
were continued for a longer time, water activity values
closely approaching unity would be attained with the con-
sequent formation of a bulk-water phase and the associated
deactivation of the enzyme. By comparison, the profiles in
Figure 8b show that capacity for water retention still exists
in the reactor (both on the Dowex resin and on the Lipo-
zyme) indicating that this run could be extended without
A model has been developed to predict quantitatively the
dynamic behavior of esterifications with immobilized en-
zymes in a continuous-flow fixed-bed reactor using organic
solvents. The dynamic behavior of these systems is com-
plicated by the interplay of reaction and adsorption phe-
nomena. Specifically, for the experimental case considered
in this work, we found that both propionic acid and water
are adsorbed onto the biocatalyst, and that adsorbed water
leads to a reversible decrease in the reaction rate. In addi-
tion, the presence of a bulk water phase together with re-
action substrates can lead to irreversible inactivation of the
enzyme. Thus, an accurate prediction of the water concen-
tration is essential for reliable operation of such reactors.
Empirically, we found that a stable operation could be
achieved if the feed of the reaction substrates is halted prior
to a significant breakthrough of water in the reactor effluent.
This result was confirmed by model simulations which
showed that when the reactor was operated in this manner,
the thermodynamic activity of water was maintained within
a value adequate to prevent formation of a bulk-water phase
throughout the reactor length.
Use of dry Dowex HCR-W2 resin in Na-form as a selec-
tive water adsorbent, packed together with the immobilized
enzyme particles in a column, was shown to result in a
substantial reduction in the accumulation of water on the
biocatalyst. In particular, the resin could be used as an ef-
fective way of controling water accumulation and thereby
obtaining higher conversion and longer cycle times. Essen-
tially, complete removal of water adsorbed both on the cata-
lyst and on the Dowex resin was shown to be possible by
flushing the reactor with pure isoamyl alcohol or with iso-
amyl alcohol containing a desired amount of water to es-
tablish a chosen level of hydration of the catalyst. The
model developed also successfully predicts the important
features of the effluent profiles obtained during isoamyl
alcohol regeneration. Thus, it can be used to predict opti-
mum reaction/regeneration cycles. Because the model is
based on a readily generalizable approach, it should also be
useful for other systems of this type.
